# shadcn/ui

shadcn/ui is an open-source, open-code component distribution platform for building custom React component libraries. Rather than providing a traditional installable package that you import from npm, shadcn/ui copies the actual component source code directly into your project, giving you full ownership and the freedom to read, modify, and extend every component. Built on Tailwind CSS v4 and React 19, it supports a composable, accessible component architecture backed by Radix UI or the Base UI primitives, with beautifully designed defaults using OKLCH color tokens and a semantic CSS variable theming system.

The core of shadcn/ui is the `shadcn` CLI (package `shadcn`, current version 4.6.0), which automates project initialization, component installation, theming, registry publishing, migrations, and MCP server integration. The project also ships a flat-file registry schema (`registry.json` / `registry-item.json`) that lets any developer author, host, and distribute their own component registries — which the CLI can consume at install time, enabling an ecosystem of public and private component libraries. Integration spans Next.js, Vite, React Router, TanStack Start, Astro, Laravel, Gatsby, and plain React projects, with full monorepo support via Turborepo.

---

## CLI: `shadcn init` — Initialize a project

The `init` command bootstraps a new or existing project with Tailwind CSS configuration, the `cn` utility, CSS variables, and optionally scaffolds a full monorepo structure. Accepts a template flag (`next`, `vite`, `start`, `react-router`, `laravel`, `astro`) and a preset code to apply a pre-configured design system.

```bash
# Scaffold a brand-new Next.js project with default settings
npx shadcn@latest init -t next

# Scaffold a Next.js monorepo (creates apps/web + packages/ui with Turborepo)
npx shadcn@latest init -t next --monorepo

# Initialize an existing project interactively
npx shadcn@latest init

# Initialize with a preset code (design system preset from ui.shadcn.com/create)
npx shadcn@latest init --preset a2r6bw --template next

# Initialize with no CSS variables (uses inline Tailwind utility classes instead)
npx shadcn@latest init --no-css-variables

# Initialize with RTL support enabled
npx shadcn@latest init --rtl

# Scaffold into a named subdirectory
npx shadcn@latest init --name my-app

# Output (example):
# ✔ Preflight checks.
# ✔ Verifying framework. Found Next.js.
# ✔ Validating Tailwind CSS.
# ✔ Writing components.json.
# ✔ Checking registry.
# ✔ Installing dependencies.
# ✔ Writing cn util.
# ✔ Writing CSS variables.
# ✔ Writing index.css.
# Success! Project initialization complete.
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn add` — Add components to a project

The `add` command fetches one or more components (or blocks) from the shadcn registry or any configured namespaced registry and writes the source files into your project under the paths defined in `components.json`. It handles all npm dependencies, registry dependencies, and import rewrites automatically.

```bash
# Add a single component
npx shadcn@latest add button

# Add multiple components at once
npx shadcn@latest add button card dialog input label

# Add all available components
npx shadcn@latest add --all

# Add a pre-built block (multi-file UI block)
npx shadcn@latest add login-03

# Add from a namespaced registry configured in components.json
npx shadcn@latest add @v0/dashboard
npx shadcn@latest add @acme/header @internal/auth-utils

# Add directly by URL
npx shadcn@latest add https://registry.acme.com/components/hero.json

# Dry-run: preview what would be written without making changes
npx shadcn@latest add button --dry-run

# Overwrite existing files
npx shadcn@latest add button --overwrite

# Specify a working directory (useful in monorepos)
npx shadcn@latest add card -c apps/web

# After adding button, use it in your app:
# app/page.tsx
import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button"

export default function Home() {
  return (
    <div className="flex min-h-svh items-center justify-center">
      <Button>Click me</Button>
    </div>
  )
}
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn apply` — Apply a preset to an existing project

The `apply` command applies a design-system preset (identified by a short code) to an existing project, updating CSS variables, fonts, and reinstalling UI components. Partial application is supported via `--only`.

```bash
# Apply a full preset (theme + fonts + components)
npx shadcn@latest apply a2r6bw

# Apply only the theme colors, skip component reinstall
npx shadcn@latest apply a2r6bw --only theme

# Apply only the font configuration
npx shadcn@latest apply a2r6bw --only font

# Skip confirmation prompt
npx shadcn@latest apply a2r6bw -y
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn preset` — Inspect and manage presets

The `preset` subcommands let you decode preset codes, resolve the preset from the current project, get the creation URL, or open the preset in the browser.

```bash
# Decode a preset code to see its full configuration
npx shadcn@latest preset decode a2r6bw
# Output (JSON representation of the preset):
# {
#   "style": "new-york",
#   "baseColor": "neutral",
#   "cssVariables": true,
#   ...
# }

# Output as JSON
npx shadcn@latest preset decode a2r6bw --json

# Resolve and print the current project's preset
npx shadcn@latest preset resolve
# Alias:
npx shadcn@latest preset info

# Get the ui.shadcn.com/create URL for a preset code
npx shadcn@latest preset url a2r6bw

# Open the preset in the browser
npx shadcn@latest preset open a2r6bw
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn view` — View registry items before installing

The `view` command fetches and displays the source files of a registry item without installing them, letting you preview what would be added to your project.

```bash
# View a single component
npx shadcn@latest view button

# View multiple items at once
npx shadcn@latest view button card dialog

# View items from namespaced registries
npx shadcn@latest view @acme/auth @v0/dashboard
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn search` — Search registries for components

The `search` command queries one or more registries and lists available items. Supports pagination and text queries.

```bash
# Search the default shadcn registry interactively
npx shadcn@latest search @shadcn

# Search with a text query
npx shadcn@latest search @shadcn -q "button"

# Search multiple registries at once
npx shadcn@latest search @shadcn @v0 @acme

# Alias: list
npx shadcn@latest list @acme

# Limit results and paginate
npx shadcn@latest search @shadcn --limit 20 --offset 0
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn build` — Build a custom registry

The `build` command reads a `registry.json` file in your project and generates the flat-file JSON payloads for each item into `public/r/`, making your registry servable over HTTP to the shadcn CLI.

```bash
# Build registry with defaults (reads ./registry.json, outputs to ./public/r/)
npx shadcn@latest build

# Specify a custom registry.json path
npx shadcn@latest build ./custom-registry.json

# Specify a custom output directory
npx shadcn@latest build --output ./public/registry

# Typical package.json integration:
# {
#   "scripts": {
#     "registry:build": "shadcn build"
#   }
# }
npm run registry:build

# Generated output example:
# public/r/hello-world.json  → installable by: npx shadcn@latest add http://localhost:3000/r/hello-world.json
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn docs` — Fetch component documentation

The `docs` command retrieves the documentation and API reference for a specific component, optionally outputting as JSON for programmatic use.

```bash
# Fetch docs for the button component
npx shadcn@latest docs button

# Output as JSON
npx shadcn@latest docs button --json

# Use a specific base (radix or base)
npx shadcn@latest docs button --base radix
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn info` — Get project information

The `info` command inspects the current project's `components.json` and prints resolved configuration details: framework, Tailwind version, aliases, installed components, icon library, and base library.

```bash
# Print project info
npx shadcn@latest info

# Output as JSON (used by the shadcn skill and MCP server)
npx shadcn@latest info --json
# Output example:
# {
#   "framework": "next",
#   "tailwindVersion": 4,
#   "style": "new-york",
#   "baseColor": "neutral",
#   "base": "radix",
#   "iconLibrary": "lucide",
#   "rsc": true,
#   "tsx": true,
#   "aliases": {
#     "components": "@/components",
#     "ui": "@/components/ui",
#     "utils": "@/lib/utils",
#     "lib": "@/lib",
#     "hooks": "@/hooks"
#   }
# }
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn migrate` — Run migrations

The `migrate` command transforms existing component files to adopt new patterns: RTL support, updated Radix UI imports, or a different icon library. Accepts file paths and glob patterns to scope the migration.

```bash
# List all available migrations
npx shadcn@latest migrate --list

# Migrate all UI components to RTL (physical → logical CSS properties)
npx shadcn@latest migrate rtl
# Example transform: ml-4 → ms-4, text-left → text-start, space-x-4 → space-x-4 rtl:space-x-reverse

# Migrate a specific file to RTL
npx shadcn@latest migrate rtl src/components/ui/button.tsx

# Migrate files matching a glob
npx shadcn@latest migrate rtl "src/components/ui/**"

# Migrate Radix UI imports to the unified radix-ui package
npx shadcn@latest migrate radix
# Before: import * as DialogPrimitive from "@radix-ui/react-dialog"
# After:  import { Dialog as DialogPrimitive } from "radix-ui"

# Migrate specific files for radix
npx shadcn@latest migrate radix "src/components/ui/**"

# Migrate icon library (interactive prompt to choose new library)
npx shadcn@latest migrate icons
```

---

## CLI: `shadcn mcp init` — Initialize the MCP server

The `mcp init` command configures the shadcn Model Context Protocol server in your AI assistant editor, enabling natural-language component discovery and installation.

```bash
# Initialize for Claude Code (writes .mcp.json)
npx shadcn@latest mcp init --client claude

# Initialize for Cursor (writes .cursor/mcp.json)
npx shadcn@latest mcp init --client cursor

# Initialize for VS Code + GitHub Copilot (writes .vscode/mcp.json)
npx shadcn@latest mcp init --client vscode

# Initialize for OpenCode
npx shadcn@latest mcp init --client opencode

# Manual configuration for Claude Code (.mcp.json):
# {
#   "mcpServers": {
#     "shadcn": {
#       "command": "npx",
#       "args": ["shadcn@latest", "mcp"]
#     }
#   }
# }

# Manual configuration for Codex (~/.codex/config.toml):
# [mcp_servers.shadcn]
# command = "npx"
# args = ["shadcn@latest", "mcp"]
```

---

## `components.json` — Project configuration file

The `components.json` file is the per-project configuration consumed by the shadcn CLI. It controls the component style, Tailwind setup, RSC/TSX options, import aliases, and optional namespaced registry sources. Created by `npx shadcn@latest init`.

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://ui.shadcn.com/schema.json",
  "style": "new-york",
  "rsc": true,
  "tsx": true,
  "tailwind": {
    "config": "",
    "css": "app/globals.css",
    "baseColor": "neutral",
    "cssVariables": true,
    "prefix": ""
  },
  "iconLibrary": "lucide",
  "aliases": {
    "components": "@/components",
    "ui": "@/components/ui",
    "lib": "@/lib",
    "hooks": "@/hooks",
    "utils": "@/lib/utils"
  },
  "registries": {
    "@v0": "https://v0.dev/chat/b/{name}",
    "@acme": "https://registry.acme.com/{name}.json",
    "@private": {
      "url": "https://api.company.com/registry/{name}.json",
      "headers": {
        "Authorization": "Bearer ${REGISTRY_TOKEN}"
      },
      "params": {
        "version": "latest"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

---

## Theming — CSS variables and OKLCH tokens

shadcn/ui uses semantic CSS variable pairs (surface + `-foreground`) defined under `:root` and `.dark` and exposed to Tailwind via `@theme inline`. Changing a variable cascades across all components that use it.

```css
/* app/globals.css — Full neutral theme scaffold */
@import "tailwindcss";
@import "shadcn/tailwind.css";

@custom-variant dark (&:is(.dark *));

@theme inline {
  --color-background: var(--background);
  --color-foreground: var(--foreground);
  --color-primary: var(--primary);
  --color-primary-foreground: var(--primary-foreground);
  --color-muted: var(--muted);
  --color-muted-foreground: var(--muted-foreground);
  --color-destructive: var(--destructive);
  --color-border: var(--border);
  --color-ring: var(--ring);
  --radius-sm: calc(var(--radius) * 0.6);
  --radius-lg: var(--radius);
  --radius-xl: calc(var(--radius) * 1.4);
}

:root {
  --radius: 0.625rem;
  --background: oklch(1 0 0);
  --foreground: oklch(0.145 0 0);
  --primary: oklch(0.205 0 0);
  --primary-foreground: oklch(0.985 0 0);
  --muted: oklch(0.97 0 0);
  --muted-foreground: oklch(0.556 0 0);
  --destructive: oklch(0.577 0.245 27.325);
  --border: oklch(0.922 0 0);
  --ring: oklch(0.708 0 0);
}

.dark {
  --background: oklch(0.145 0 0);
  --foreground: oklch(0.985 0 0);
  --primary: oklch(0.922 0 0);
  --primary-foreground: oklch(0.205 0 0);
  --destructive: oklch(0.704 0.191 22.216);
  --border: oklch(1 0 0 / 10%);
  --ring: oklch(0.556 0 0);
}

/* Add a custom warning token */
:root { --warning: oklch(0.84 0.16 84); --warning-foreground: oklch(0.28 0.07 46); }
.dark  { --warning: oklch(0.41 0.11 46); --warning-foreground: oklch(0.99 0.02 95); }
@theme inline { --color-warning: var(--warning); --color-warning-foreground: var(--warning-foreground); }

/* Usage in components */
/* <div className="bg-primary text-primary-foreground">Hello</div>   */
/* <div className="bg-warning text-warning-foreground">Alert</div>   */
```

---

## Monorepo setup

The CLI natively supports Turborepo monorepos with a `packages/ui` shared component package and one or more `apps/*` consumers. Each workspace must have its own `components.json` that configures workspace-specific aliases.

```bash
# Scaffold a fresh Next.js monorepo
npx shadcn@latest init -t next --monorepo

# Add a UI component from inside the app workspace
cd apps/web
npx shadcn@latest add button
# → installs button.tsx to packages/ui/src/components/
# → imports automatically rewritten to @workspace/ui/components/button

# Add a block (only the page-level component goes to apps/web)
npx shadcn@latest add login-01
# → button, input, label, card → packages/ui/src/components/
# → login-form.tsx → apps/web/components/
```

```json
// apps/web/components.json
{
  "$schema": "https://ui.shadcn.com/schema.json",
  "style": "radix-nova",
  "rsc": true,
  "tsx": true,
  "tailwind": { "config": "", "css": "../../packages/ui/src/styles/globals.css", "baseColor": "neutral", "cssVariables": true },
  "iconLibrary": "lucide",
  "aliases": {
    "components": "@/components",
    "hooks": "@/hooks",
    "lib": "@/lib",
    "utils": "@workspace/ui/lib/utils",
    "ui": "@workspace/ui/components"
  }
}
```

```tsx
// Consuming shared components in apps/web
import { Button } from "@workspace/ui/components/button"
import { cn } from "@workspace/ui/lib/utils"
import { useTheme } from "@workspace/ui/hooks/use-theme"

export default function Page() {
  return <Button className={cn("mt-4")}>Get started</Button>
}
```

---

## Registry authoring — `registry.json` and `registry-item.json`

Developers can publish their own shadcn-compatible registries. Define items in `registry.json`, run `shadcn build` to generate flat JSON payloads, and deploy to any HTTP host.

```json
// registry.json — entry point for your registry
{
  "$schema": "https://ui.shadcn.com/schema/registry.json",
  "name": "acme",
  "homepage": "https://acme.com",
  "items": [
    {
      "name": "hello-world",
      "type": "registry:block",
      "title": "Hello World",
      "description": "A simple hello world component.",
      "registryDependencies": ["button"],
      "dependencies": ["motion"],
      "devDependencies": ["tw-animate-css"],
      "files": [
        {
          "path": "registry/new-york/hello-world/hello-world.tsx",
          "type": "registry:component"
        },
        {
          "path": "registry/new-york/hello-world/use-hello-world.ts",
          "type": "registry:hook"
        },
        {
          "path": "registry/new-york/hello-world/page.tsx",
          "type": "registry:page",
          "target": "app/hello/page.tsx"
        }
      ],
      "cssVars": {
        "theme": { "font-heading": "Poppins, sans-serif" },
        "light": { "brand": "oklch(0.205 0.015 18)" },
        "dark":  { "brand": "oklch(0.205 0.015 18)" }
      },
      "envVars": {
        "NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL": "http://localhost:3000"
      },
      "docs": "Visit https://acme.com/hello-world for usage examples."
    }
  ]
}
```

```bash
# Build and serve your registry
npm run registry:build        # → generates public/r/hello-world.json

# Install from your local registry during development
npx shadcn@latest add http://localhost:3000/r/hello-world.json

# Install from your published registry
npx shadcn@latest add https://acme.com/r/hello-world.json
```

---

## Registry content negotiation — root-hosted registries

The CLI sends `Accept: application/vnd.shadcn.v1+json` and `User-Agent: shadcn` headers. Servers can use this to serve HTML to browsers and JSON to the CLI from the same URL.

```typescript
// next.config.ts — serve registry JSON to the CLI at domain root
import type { NextConfig } from "next"

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  async rewrites() {
    return {
      beforeFiles: [
        {
          source: "/",
          has: [{ type: "header", key: "accept", value: "(.*)application/vnd\\.shadcn\\.v1\\+json(.*)" }],
          destination: "/r/index.json",
        },
        {
          source: "/",
          has: [{ type: "header", key: "user-agent", value: "shadcn" }],
          destination: "/r/index.json",
        },
      ],
    }
  },
  async headers() {
    return [{ source: "/", headers: [{ key: "Vary", value: "Accept, User-Agent" }] }]
  },
}

export default nextConfig

// Users can now install with: npx shadcn@latest add https://ui.example.com
```

---

## Namespaced registries — private and third-party sources

`components.json` supports a `registries` map that assigns short `@namespace` prefixes to registry URLs (with optional auth headers and query params). Once configured, components from those registries are installed using the `@namespace/name` syntax.

```json
// components.json — configure multiple registries
{
  "registries": {
    "@shadcn":     "https://ui.shadcn.com/r/{name}.json",
    "@v0":         "https://v0.dev/chat/b/{name}",
    "@company-ui": {
      "url": "https://registry.company.com/ui/{name}.json",
      "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer ${COMPANY_TOKEN}" }
    },
    "@team": {
      "url": "https://team.company.com/{name}.json",
      "params": { "team": "frontend", "version": "${REGISTRY_VERSION}" }
    }
  }
}
```

```bash
# .env.local — supply credentials
COMPANY_TOKEN=your_token_here
REGISTRY_VERSION=stable

# Install from any configured registry
npx shadcn@latest add @company-ui/button
npx shadcn@latest add @team/auth-form
npx shadcn@latest add @v0/dashboard @shadcn/card

# Search across multiple registries
npx shadcn@latest search @shadcn @company-ui -q "form"
```

---

## Tailwind v4 upgrade — removing `forwardRef` and updating CSS

shadcn/ui v4 components drop `React.forwardRef` in favor of direct `React.ComponentProps` and add `data-slot` attributes to every primitive for Tailwind-based styling.

```tsx
// Before (Tailwind v3 / React 18 pattern)
const AccordionItem = React.forwardRef<
  React.ElementRef<typeof AccordionPrimitive.Item>,
  React.ComponentPropsWithoutRef<typeof AccordionPrimitive.Item>
>(({ className, ...props }, ref) => (
  <AccordionPrimitive.Item
    ref={ref}
    className={cn("border-b last:border-b-0", className)}
    {...props}
  />
))
AccordionItem.displayName = "AccordionItem"

// After (Tailwind v4 / React 19 pattern)
function AccordionItem({
  className,
  ...props
}: React.ComponentProps<typeof AccordionPrimitive.Item>) {
  return (
    <AccordionPrimitive.Item
      data-slot="accordion-item"
      className={cn("border-b last:border-b-0", className)}
      {...props}
    />
  )
}

// Tailwind v4 CSS variable migration
// Before:
// @layer base { :root { --background: 0 0% 100%; } }
// @theme { --color-background: hsl(var(--background)); }

// After (with @theme inline):
:root  { --background: oklch(1 0 0); }
.dark  { --background: oklch(0.145 0 0); }
@theme inline { --color-background: var(--background); }
```

---

## Skills — AI assistant integration

The shadcn skill gives AI assistants (Claude Code, etc.) project-aware context by running `shadcn info --json` on each interaction and injecting the resolved project configuration, component inventory, aliases, and CLI reference into the assistant's context window.

```bash
# Install the shadcn skill into your project
npx skills add shadcn/ui

# The skill auto-detects projects with a components.json file.
# Example prompts once installed:
# "Add a login form with email and password fields."
# "Create a settings page with profile update form."
# "Build a dashboard with a sidebar, stats cards, and data table."
# "Switch to --preset a2r6bw"
# "Can you add a hero section from @tailark?"
```

---

shadcn/ui is used primarily in two scenarios: as a starting point for building design systems in greenfield React applications, and as an incremental adoption tool for adding accessible, well-styled components to existing projects. In the first scenario, `shadcn init` bootstraps a complete project scaffold (single app or monorepo), the preset system lets teams encode their brand tokens into a shareable code, and the registry system lets platform teams publish internal component libraries that product teams consume with a single `add` command. In the second scenario, individual components are cherry-picked and installed into an existing Tailwind project without requiring a wholesale migration, and migrations (`migrate rtl`, `migrate radix`) handle the mechanical transformation work.

Integration patterns center on three entry points: the CLI for human-driven workflows, the MCP server for AI-driven workflows (Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code Copilot), and the Skills system for giving AI assistants deep project context. The registry spec (`registry.json` + `registry-item.json`) acts as the common interchange format for all three: the CLI installs from it, the MCP server browses and installs from it, and AI assistants can generate conformant registry items to distribute new components. Teams building internal design systems typically pair a hosted registry (deployed Next.js or Express app serving `/r/*.json`) with `components.json` namespace configuration so developers can install internal components with the same `npx shadcn@latest add @company/button` UX as the public registry.
